mnemonics.title: LRN - Left Right Node
mnemonics.content: Remember traversal orders with first letter of operations: PRE-order = R (Root first), IN-order = N (Node in middle), POST-order = L (Leaf/left first). Think: 'Rhyme with PRehistoric - Root comes PRE'

mnemonics.title: Queue for Level - Stack for Depth
mnemonics.content: Level-order needs Queue (FIFO) to process siblings together. Depth-first (pre/in/post) uses Stack (LIFO) or recursion to go deep before wide.

mnemonics.title: Deep Copy = New Nodes for All
mnemonics.content: For deep copy: ALWAYS create NEW node using 'new' or malloc. For shallow: just copy the pointer addresses. Think: 'Deep = Allocate fresh memory for EVERY node'.

acronyms.usage: Refers to pre-order, in-order, post-order traversals that explore depth before breadth
Same as level-order traversal - explores level by level
Binary tree with left < root < right property, enabling O(log n) search
Stack behavior used in recursive/implicit tree traversal
Queue behavior used in level-order traversal

visualTips.visualization: Draw three identical trees side by side. Circle root, square left subtree, triangle right subtree. Label arrows: Pre=1-2-3, In=2-1-3, Post=2-3-1 showing order of visiting R-L-N nodes.
Draw queue as horizontal boxes. Push root, process it, add children to back. Show how queue front slides right as we process, children add to back, creating wave effect down levels.

Draw original tree nodes as circles with arrows. For shallow: draw same circles with new arrows pointing to them. For deep: draw entirely NEW circles with same values but different physical locations.

analogies.analogy: Think of traversing a company's org chart: Pre-order = report to boss first, then subordinates. Post-order = check all subordinates before reporting to boss. In-order = alphabetical by name within each level.
Queue is like a line at a ticket counter. First person (root) gets served, their children (if any) join the end of the line. People are processed in the order they arrived - level by level.
Deep copy is like making a photocopy of a document - you get a completely independent copy. Shallow copy is like giving someone the original file - they can access and modify the same document.
In-order on BST is like reading pages of a book in order - pages (nodes) are organized such that scanning left-to-right naturally gives sorted sequence, just as in-order visits left-root-right.
